text: This video represents a case of a twenty-year-old pregnant female who underwent right atrial aneurysm excision via mid-sternotomy in two thousand thirteen. In October twenty twenty, she started complaining of chest heaviness. A two by four centimeter anterior mediastinal mass was incidentally detected and confirmed by chest CT. Therefore, she was referred for surgical intervention.

A thoracoscopic port is inserted anteriorly at the right eighth intercostal space to explore the anterior mediastinal area for severe adhesions. A utility incision is made at the subxiphoid region, with visible evidence of the previously opened pericardium from past surgery.

Through the thoracoscopic view from the right eighth intercostal space, the surgeon performs retro sternum dissection, lysing postoperative adhesions. Thymic tissue adjacent to the superior vena cava and phrenic nerve is excised. The innominate vein is identified and dissected, with the thymic vein divided using a harmonic scalpel. Sutures from previous surgery are visible during dissection along the innominate vein, and the thymocorn is removed.

The surgeon identifies another thymic vein and clips it using a polymer clip, then divides it with a harmonic scalpel. The remaining thymic tissue is dissected from the left pleura, and the diaphragmatic fat is shown being dissected.

The surgeon retrieves the resected mass from the thoracic cavity, which weighs 62 grams and exhibits features of lymphocyte-rich thymoma B-II. A single chest tube is inserted through the intercostal incision, and the subxiphoid incision is closed. The patient is extubated in the operating room and transferred to the ICU in stable condition.
